mappings.rationaleAI: British colonial rule in Myanmar through the Anglo-Burmese Wars and annexation to British India (A.1.a), and British intrusions into Malaya and assumption of power from sultanates (A.1.b) are directly covered.

The Spanish Empire's rivalry with the Portuguese in the Spice Islands (B.1.a), Spanish control of the Philippines from 1571 to 1898 including government administration and the influence of the Catholic Church (B.1.b).
Myanmar's Konbaung dynasty, the three Anglo-Burmese Wars, annexation to British India (1886), and effects of British colonialism (A.1.a.i–ii) are comprehensively covered.

Thailand's (Siam's) trade relations, Theravada Buddhism, Dutch and French intrusions, Myanmar's invasion (1767), the Chakkri dynasty, Mongkut's reign, and Chulalongkorn's reforms (A.3.a–c) are directly covered.
Vietnam's history of Portuguese and French missionary involvement, Vietnamese reaction (A.2.a), and French colonial territorial acquisition and administration in Indochina (A.2.b) are covered.
Singapore's evolution from British colonial rule (from 1819) connects to British incursions into Malaya in the late 18th century and subsequent assumption of power (A.1.b.ii).

The Philippines' pre-colonial culture (B.1.b.i), Spanish control including government administration and the Church (B.1.b.ii), and the Philippine Revolution and US takeover (B.1.b.iii) are comprehensively covered.
US support in ousting the Spanish from the Philippines and subsequent US administration (B.1.b.iii) connects to the American role in the Philippines.
Jakarta's history as the Dutch East India Company's Batavia (1619–1942) directly connects to Coen's establishment of Dutch commercial supremacy (B.2.a).

European colonial domination through mercantilism and chartered companies (Dutch East India Company) frames the entire section on mainland Southeast Asian states (A) and Indonesian Archipelago and Philippines (B).
The extension and maintenance of European power over Southeast Asian territories frames the section's treatment of British, French, Dutch, and Spanish imperial presence across the region (A, B).

Asian history including European colonialism, South and Southeast Asian kingdoms, and the Great Game provides broad context for both mainland (A) and archipelagic (B) Southeast Asia under European imperialism.

Malaysia's Malay sultanates, British colonialism from the 18th century, and Chinese immigration connect to Dutch intervention in the Malay states (A.1.b.i) and British incursions and assumption of power (A.1.b.ii).
Portuguese maritime exploration and domination of the Strait of Malacca connect to Portuguese naval and commercial activities and rivalry with the Spanish in the Spice Islands (B.1.a).

Indonesia's Dutch colonial rule spanning three and a half centuries directly covers the Dutch East India Company (B.2.a), French and British in Java (B.2.b), and the Culture System and Liberal Policy (B.2.b.i).
France's colonial empire including Indochina directly covers French missionary involvement in Vietnam, intervention, and territorial acquisition of Cochinchina and Cambodia (A.2.a–b).
Spain's imperial legacy in the Philippines from 1571 to 1898 covers Spanish control including government administration, the Church, land policy, and the rise of nationalism (B.1.b.ii).
The early modern period's European colonization of Asia provides broad context for Portuguese and Spanish commercial activities (B.1.a) and European states' influence in mainland Southeast Asia (A).
China's trade relations with Siam and other Asian countries connect to Siam's trade relations with China and Chinese immigration (A.3.a).
